flashcards.front: Define Sequential Search (Linear Search) and state its time complexity for n elements in worst, best, and average cases.
flashcards.back: Sequential Search examines each element of a list sequentially until the target is found or the list is exhausted. Worst-case: O(n) - target at last position or not present. Best-case: O(1) - target at first position. Average-case: O(n/2) = O(n) - target equally likely at any position.

flashcards.front: What is the loop invariant for Sequential Search?
flashcards.back: At the start of each iteration of the while loop, all elements at indices 0 through (i-1) have been examined and do not contain the search key. If the loop terminates with index i, either i = n (not found) or A[i] = key (found).

flashcards.front: Define Brute Force String Matching and state its worst-case time complexity.
flashcards.back: Brute Force String Matching compares the pattern P[0...m-1] with the text T[0...n-1] at all possible starting positions. For each shift s from 0 to n-m, it compares characters sequentially. Worst-case time complexity: O((n-m+1) × m) = O(nm), where n is text length and m is pattern length.

flashcards.front: What is the best-case time complexity for Brute Force String Matching?
flashcards.back: Best-case: O(n-m+1) = O(n) when the pattern length is m. This occurs when the first character of the pattern does not match any character in the text, so only one comparison per shift is needed.

flashcards.front: When does the worst-case of Brute Force String Matching occur?
flashcards.back: Worst-case occurs when: (1) The pattern is like 'aaaa...a' and text is 'aaaa...a' (all matches except last character), or (2) The pattern and text both consist of repeated patterns that cause maximum character comparisons at each shift. In this case, approximately (n-m+1) × m comparisons are made.

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity of Sequential Search and Brute Force String Matching?
flashcards.back: Both algorithms have O(1) space complexity. They use only a constant number of variables regardless of input size - sequential search uses indices i and n; brute force string matching uses indices i, j, s.

flashcards.front: Explain the average-case analysis of Sequential Search with formal derivation.
flashcards.back: If the search key is present and equally likely to be at any of the n positions, the average number of comparisons = (1+2+...+n)/n = n(n+1)/(2n) = (n+1)/2. If not present, exactly n comparisons are needed. Thus average case is O(n).

flashcards.front: What is the average-case time complexity for Brute Force String Matching?
flashcards.back: Assuming alphabet of size σ and random text/pattern, average comparisons per shift is approximately m(1 - 1/σ^m)/(1 - 1/σ) ≈ m/σ for large σ. For typical text, average complexity approaches O(n). For worst-case patterns like 'aaa...a', average approaches worst-case O(nm).

flashcards.front: State and prove the correctness of Sequential Search using loop invariant.
flashcards.back: Loop Invariant: After j iterations, the algorithm has examined elements T[0] through T[j-1]. Proof: (1) Initialization: j=0, no elements examined - true. (2) Maintenance: If T[j] ≠ key, increment j; elements 0 to j-1 are examined. (3) Termination: Either key found at index j or j=n (exhausted). In both cases, algorithm is correct.

flashcards.front: Why might Brute Force String Matching be preferred despite O(nm) complexity?
flashcards.back: Brute Force is preferred when: (1) Pattern length m is small relative to text, (2) Alphabet size σ is large (reduces average comparisons), (3) Simplicity and ease of implementation needed, (4) No preprocessing of pattern required, (5) Hardware-level string operations can optimize it.

flashcards.front: Derive the worst-case number of character comparisons in Brute Force String Matching.
flashcards.back: Worst-case occurs when pattern almost matches at each shift. For n-m+1 shifts, each shift makes up to m comparisons. Total comparisons = Σ_{s=0}^{n-m} m = (n-m+1) × m ≈ nm for n >> m. In Big-O notation, this is O(nm).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between internal and external searching in the context of Sequential Search?
flashcards.back: Internal searching: All records fit in main memory (RAM), direct array access. External searching: Records stored on external storage (disk), requires I/O considerations. Sequential search is more practical for external searching when records are stored in blocks.
